title: Day 1: Why I started building Interlock
x_text:
Day 1 building Interlock: AI agents are starting to call real tools, not just generate text. That means prompt injection can become tool misuse, data leaks, or bad actions.

So I started building a runtime security gateway for AI agents.

#AISecurity #AIAgents #MCP
reddit_title: Day 1 building Interlock: why AI agents need a runtime security gateway
reddit_text:
AI agents are moving from chat into real tool use: Slack, databases, files, payment APIs, CRMs, MCP servers. That changes the risk model. Prompt injection is no longer just a weird answer; it can become a tool call, a data leak, or an unauthorized action.

That is why I started building Interlock: a runtime security gateway for AI agents. The goal is to inspect prompts, tool calls, MCP server behavior, and outputs before risky actions happen.

I am sharing the build in public and would love feedback from devs/security folks building with agents or MCP.

title: Day 2: MCP is the security boundary
x_text:
Day 2 building Interlock: the interesting part is not a generic prompt filter.

The real boundary is MCP: which tools exist, which agents can call them, what changed, and what gets logged.

That is where Interlock is focused.

#MCP #AISecurity #DevSecOps
reddit_title: Day 2 building Interlock: MCP is the security boundary
reddit_text:
The more I worked on Interlock, the more obvious it became that the valuable part is not just a generic prompt filter. The important boundary is MCP and tool execution.

Questions I want Interlock to answer:

- Which MCP servers are registered?
- Which tools did they expose at baseline?
- Did a tool schema or description drift later?
- Which agent role is allowed to call it?
- Was a risky call blocked, monitored, or quarantined?

That is the core product direction.

title: Day 3: The dashboard needs to tell a security story
x_text:
Day 3 building Interlock: a security dashboard has to be instantly judgeable.

Usage, MCP servers, drift/quarantine, scan results, and audit history should be visible without digging through logs.

That is what I am shaping now.

#DevSecOps #AISecurity #MCP
reddit_title: Day 3 building Interlock: making the dashboard useful for CTOs and devs
reddit_text:
Today’s focus was the dashboard. I do not want it to be a vanity analytics screen. It has to help a CTO, security engineer, or developer quickly answer: is this agent/tool stack safe enough to run?

The current dashboard shows usage, MCP servers, drift/quarantine state, shadow findings, quick scan actions, demo prompts, and audit evidence.

The next thing I am improving is making risk posture easier to judge visually.

title: Day 4: Demo prompts are part of the product
x_text:
Day 4 building Interlock: demo prompts matter.

Safe baseline, prompt injection, jailbreak, fake PII, leetspeak bypass, output leak scan.

A buyer should be able to test the product in 60 seconds.

#AISecurity #AIAgents #MCP
reddit_title: Day 4 building Interlock: demo prompts make the product easier to evaluate
reddit_text:
One lesson from building Interlock: demo prompts are part of the product.

If someone opens the dashboard, they should not have to invent test cases. They should be able to click safe baseline, prompt injection, jailbreak attempt, fake PII payload, leetspeak bypass, and output leak scan.

The goal is to make evaluation fast: run the examples, see what gets blocked, and inspect the audit evidence.

title: Day 5: Audit evidence over vibes
x_text:
Day 5 building Interlock: security tools need evidence, not vibes.

Every allow/block/monitor/quarantine decision should be visible: source, target, reason, severity, scan time, and policy context.

That audit layer is becoming central.

#DevSecOps #AISecurity #MCP
reddit_title: Day 5 building Interlock: audit evidence over vibes
reddit_text:
A runtime security gateway is not useful if it only says “blocked” or “allowed”. You need evidence.

For Interlock, I am making audit logs central: prompt scans, output scans, MCP decisions, action, severity, reason, scan time, and source.

The design goal is that a team can review what happened without digging through raw backend logs.

title: Day 6: Response scanning matters too
x_text:
Day 6 building Interlock: input scanning is not enough.

Agent/tool responses can leak PII, secrets, or injected instructions too. So Interlock scans outputs before they move downstream.

Runtime control has to cover both directions.

#AISecurity #DevSecOps #AIAgents
reddit_title: Day 6 building Interlock: output scanning matters too
reddit_text:
Input scanning is only half the story. Agent and tool outputs can contain PII, secrets, injected instructions, or unsafe data that should not be forwarded downstream.

That is why Interlock has output scanning in addition to prompt/tool-call checks.

The product direction is bidirectional runtime control: inspect what goes into the agent/tool stack and what comes out of it.

title: Day 7: MCP drift is a real signal
x_text:
Day 7 building Interlock: MCP drift is a security signal.

If a tool changes its schema, description, side effects, or data classes after baseline, teams should know before agents keep using it.

Monitor, block, or quarantine.

#MCP #AISecurity #DevSecOps
reddit_title: Day 7 building Interlock: MCP drift is a real security signal
reddit_text:
MCP tools are not static. A server can expose a different schema, description, side effect, or data class after baseline. That matters if agents keep calling the tool automatically.

Interlock treats drift as a first-class signal. The intended flow is: discover baseline, compare future tool definitions, classify drift severity, and then allow/monitor/block/quarantine based on policy.

This is one of the areas I think matters most for agent security.

title: Day 8: Agent roles need permissions
x_text:
Day 8 building Interlock: agents need RBAC too.

A support agent, finance agent, devops agent, and readonly analyst should not have the same tool permissions.

Interlock checks role + tool + args before execution.

#AIAgents #DevSecOps #MCP
reddit_title: Day 8 building Interlock: agent roles need permissions too
reddit_text:
A support agent, finance agent, devops agent, readonly analyst, and admin agent should not have the same tool permissions.

One of the pieces inside Interlock is role-aware policy for agent tool use. The goal is to evaluate role + tool + arguments before execution.

This matters because agent mistakes often happen at the tool boundary, not only in the model response.

title: Day 9: Positioning Interlock clearly
x_text:
Day 9 building Interlock: positioning matters.

I do not want to pitch this as another generic filter.

Interlock is a runtime security gateway for AI agents, with MCP gateway, RBAC, drift detection, output scanning, and audit evidence.

#AISecurity #MCP #BuildInPublic
reddit_title: Day 9 building Interlock: positioning the product clearly
reddit_text:
A big part of building Interlock has been positioning. I do not want it to sound like another generic prompt filter.

The product is a runtime security gateway for AI agents. The core is MCP gateway behavior: tool baselines, drift detection, role-aware policy, output scanning, and audit evidence.

That positioning has made the roadmap clearer.

title: Day 10: Where Interlock is now
x_text:
Day 10 building Interlock: current state.

Landing page, dashboard, scan UI, demo prompts, MCP visibility, audit timeline, risk visuals, and social launch automation are now in place.

Next: pilots, sharper tests, and real user feedback.

#AISecurity #MCP #DevSecOps
reddit_title: Day 10 building Interlock: where the project is now
reddit_text:
Current state of Interlock: landing page, dashboard, scan UI, demo prompts, MCP visibility, audit timeline, risk visuals, and social launch automation are now in place.

The next work is less about making it look finished and more about making it survive real usage: pilots, sharper tests, backend reliability, and honest feedback from developers/security teams.

If you were evaluating this as a CTO or security lead, what would you want to see next?
